concepts.title: Symmetric and skew symmetric
concepts.conceptBody:
Symmetric Matrices

A square matrix $A=\left[ {{a}_{ij}} \right]$ will be called symmetric it for all values of $i$ and $j,{{a}_{ij}}={{a}_{jj}}$.

i.e. every $\text{i}$-jth element $=\text{j}$-ith element.

eg. $A={{\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} a & h & g \\ h & b & f \\ g & f & c \\ \end{array} \right]}_{3\times 3}}$

Property $\mathbf{A}'\mathbf{=A}$ 

Skew Symmetric Matrix:

A square matrix $A=\left[ {{a}_{ij}} \right]$ wil be called skew symmetric if its $i-j$ th element is -ive of $j$-ith element for all values of $i$ and $j$ i.e. $an=$ an for all values of $i$ and $j$.

Since diagonal elements will be of the type ${{a}_{11}}\cdot {{a}_{22}}\cdot {{a}_{33}},\ldots {{a}_{n}}$ and by given condition ${{a}_{n}}=-{{a}_{n}}$ for all values of $i$ or

$2{{\text{a}}_{\text{ii}}}=0\therefore \quad {{\text{a}}_{\text{ii}}}=\mathbf{0}$

Hence the diagonal elements of a skew symmetric matrix are zero.

$\text{ e}\text{.g}\text{. }\left[ \begin{matrix} 0 & h & g \\ -h & 0 & f \\ -g & -f & 0 \\ \end{matrix} \right]\text{ is a skew symmetric matrix}\text{. }$

Property: ${{A}^{\prime }}=-A$.

Some results related to symmetric and skew symmetric matrices:

(i) If $\text{A}$ is any square matrix, then $\text{A}+{{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$ is a symmetric matrix and $\text{A}-{{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$ is a skew symmetric matrix. $A-{{A}^{\prime }}$ is a skew symmetric matrix.

Proof: $\left( A+{{A}^{\prime }} \right)={{A}^{\prime }}+\left( {{A}^{\prime }} \right)={{A}^{\prime }}+A+{{A}^{\prime }}\left[ \therefore {{(A+B)}^{\prime }}\left( {{A}^{\prime }}+{{B}^{\prime }} \right] \right.$

Hence $\text{A}+{{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$ is a symmetric matrix.

Again $\left( A-{{A}^{\prime }} \right)={{A}^{\prime }}-\left( {{A}^{\prime }} \right)={{A}^{\prime }}-A=-\left( A-{{A}^{\prime }} \right)$

Hence $\text{A}-{{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$ is a skew symmetric matrix

(ii) Every square matrix can be uniquely expressed as the sum of asymmetric matrix and askew symmetric matrix .

Proof: Let A be any square matrix. Then as in (i) $\frac{1}{2}\left( A+{{A}^{\prime }} \right)$ will be a symmetric matrix and $\frac{1}{2}\left( A-{{A}^{\prime }} \right)$ will be a skew symmetric matrix.

$\text{ Let }B=\frac{1}{2}\left( A+{{A}^{\prime }} \right)\text{ and }C=\frac{1}{2}\left( A-{{A}^{\prime }} \right)$

$\text{ Then }A=\frac{1}{2}\left( A+{{A}^{\prime }} \right)+\frac{1}{2}\left( A-{{A}^{\prime }} \right)=B+C$

where $\text{B}$ is a symmetric matrix and $\text{C}$ is a skew symmetric matrix.

To prove that the representation is unique: 

If possible let $\text{A}=\text{D}+\text{E}$ where $\text{D}$ is a symmetric and $\text{E}$ is a skew symmetric matrix. Then ${{\text{D}}^{\prime }}={{\text{D}}_{\text{and }}}{{\text{E}}^{\prime }}=-\text{E}$. ...(1)

Now $\text{A}=\text{D}+\text{E}\Rightarrow {{\text{A}}^{\prime }}={{\text{D}}^{\prime }}+{{\text{E}}^{\prime }}=\text{D}-\text{E}[$ From (1)]

$\left. \begin{matrix} \text{ Thus }A & =\text{D}+\text{E} \\ \text{ and }{{\text{A}}^{\prime }} & =\text{D}-\text{E} \\ \end{matrix} \right\}\text{ and E}=\frac{1}{2}\left( ~\text{A}-{{\text{A}}^{\prime }} \right)=\text{C}\text{. }$
